---
title: "Missing Data Tutorial"
author: "Colin Madland"
date: "2026-02-20"
categories: [missingness, missing data]
execute:
    freeze: true
image: megadeth.jpg
---

::: {.callout}

I'm working through the tutorial published by M. Fatih Tüzen at [A Statistician's R Notebook](https://mfatihtuzen.netlify.app/posts/2025-08-18_missing_values/), and syndicated at [R-Bloggers](https://www.r-bloggers.com/2025/08/handling-missing-data-in-r-a-comprehensive-guide/)
::: 

Missing data are important, and dealing with them appropriately is critical to avoid the bias that might be introduced if they are ignored or improperly dealt with. Missing values decrease statistical power and compromise the validity of the analysis and conclusions.

First, we load the required libraries. Click or tap 'Show the code' to view and/or copy the code block. Within the code block, you will see that each function is linked to the documentation page for that function.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(NHANES)
library(tidyverse)
library(naniar)
library(VIM)
library(mice)
library(missForest)
library(grateful)
```
:::


The `NHANES` dataset, from the US National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey is very large, so the tutorial will only use a subset of variables:

ID
: Unique identifier for each participant

Age
: Age of the participant

Gender
: Biological sex (male or female)

BMI
: Body Mass Index

BPSysAve
: Average systolic blood pressure

Diabetes
: Whether the participant has been diagnosed with diabetes

Take a glimpse at a subset of the `NHANES` dataset...


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Select relevant variables from NHANES dataset
nhanes_sub <- NHANES |> 
  select(ID, Age, Gender, BMI, BPSysAve, Diabetes)
glimpse(nhanes_sub)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Rows: 10,000
Columns: 6
$ ID       <int> 51624, 51624, 51624, 51625, 51630, 51638, 51646, 51647, 51647…
$ Age      <int> 34, 34, 34, 4, 49, 9, 8, 45, 45, 45, 66, 58, 54, 10, 58, 50, …
$ Gender   <fct> male, male, male, male, female, male, male, female, female, f…
$ BMI      <dbl> 32.22, 32.22, 32.22, 15.30, 30.57, 16.82, 20.64, 27.24, 27.24…
$ BPSysAve <int> 113, 113, 113, NA, 112, 86, 107, 118, 118, 118, 111, 104, 134…
$ Diabetes <fct> No, No, No, No, No, No, No, No, No, No, No, No, No, No, No, N…
```


:::
:::
